items.question: How does L1 distance compare to L2 distance for the same pair of vectors?
items.answer: L1 sums absolute coordinate differences; L2 takes the square root of summed squared differences. L1 is less sensitive to a single large outlier coordinate in relative terms but treats all dimensions symmetrically in absolute terms; L2 penalizes large coordinate gaps more aggressively due to squaring.

items.question: When should you prefer cosine similarity over Euclidean distance on embeddings?
items.answer: Prefer cosine when vector magnitude carries little semantic meaning (e.g. variable-length text embeddings) and direction encodes content. Prefer Euclidean when both direction and magnitude matter, or after normalizing vectors so L2 and cosine rankings align.

items.question: What if you double every feature value before running k-NN with L2 distance?
items.answer: All distances scale by a factor of 2, so neighbor rankings stay the same—but if only some features are doubled, those features dominate distance and neighbors change accordingly.

items.question: What if two vectors are orthogonal (dot product zero)?
items.answer: Their cosine similarity is 0 (perpendicular directions). Their L2 distance is sqrt(||u||₂² + ||v||₂²) by the Pythagorean theorem, regardless of whether each vector is unit length.
